metadata.title: Mining halal food search pathways down the Wikipedia’s rabbit hole

abstract: Wikipedia has grown to be the biggest online encyclopedia in terms of comprehensiveness, reach, and coverage. However, although different websites and social network platforms such as Twitter, Facebook, and Reddit have received considerable academic attention, Wikipedia has largely gone unnoticed. This is surprising given the fact that Wikipedia is the fifth most-widely visited website. In this study, we fill this research gap by investigating halal food navigation strategies over an entire month. We use the recently released clickstream data which contains more than 25 million pairs of URLs in English language, accounting for a total of around 6-billion-page requests to the Wikipedia servers. Compared to outgoing traffic analysis, search patterns of the incoming traffic to the halal food entry reveal that readers are more prone to fall into the Wikipedia rabbit hole. Using techniques borrowed from social network analysis (SNA), our results also show that the information network of the Wikipedia halal food topics follows a small world preferential attachment pattern. This finding lends strong support to the "Matthew Effect", which is a variation of the "rich get richer" model. Our findings hold important implications for different stakeholders as they underscore the relational nature of halal food networks and the importance of online encyclopedias as critical information tools.

sections.paper_id: Mostafa - 2022 - Mining halal food search pathways down the Wikipedia’s rabbit hole
paragraphs: Muslims usually strive to obtain and consume halal food. Halal is an Arabic phrase that can be roughly translated as allowed or permitted (Lubis et al. 2016). Halal rules for animal slaughter "are based on the dual principles of effectively draining the animal's blood, without inflicting unnecessary suffering" (Kocturk 2002, 139). Since halal has become a huge globalized market (Mostafa 2018(Mostafa , 2019)), there has been recently a phenomenal growth in halal products and brands. This includes areas as diverse as finance, banking, pharmaceuticals, textile, cosmetics, fashion, schools, media outlets, and tourism (Yener 2015). By 2030, the global halal market size is estimated to reach US$ 10 trillion (Ali, Ali, and Sherwani 2017). Due to the growing Muslim population worldwide, global halal food currently comprises around 17% of the total market (Ali and Suleiman 2016). Talib et al. (2016) found that halal food market is expanding quite rapidly even in non-Muslim majority countries, especially in Europe, Asia-Pacific, and the Americas. Currently, almost all fast-food restaurants as well as leading retailers do offer halal food choice in their menu (Awan and Haidar 2015). Although there is a general consensus among scholars that halal food research is "still in its infancy" (Aziz and Chok 2013, 5), existing research has examined several aspects of halal food using several platforms (Salman and Sidiqui 2011). However, to the best of our knowledge, no previous research has examined halal food using Wikipedia.

Wikipedia is considered the largest multilingual web encyclopedia, where a large number of common users regularly update and add new articles. Azad and Deepak (2019, 150) argued that "the exponential growth and reliability of Wikipedia make it an ideal knowledge resource for information retrieval." Wikipedia was launched in 2001 and since then it has grown to include more than thirty-five million articles in around 300 languages, making it the fifth most-widely visited website (Gobel and Munzert 2018). Each month, Wikipedia welcomes 400 million unique visitors, nearly half of them visit the English edition (Navarrete and Borowiecki 2016). Due to its exponential growth over time, the encyclopedia is structured as a complex network of articles. In this interconnected network, each article focuses on a single concept and includes hyperlinks connecting to other articles either inside or outside the Wikipedia encyclopedia. Hyperlinks are designed to direct the interested reader to more in-depth descriptions of the major notions mentioned in the articles. Wikipedia hyperlinks have been constantly validated for semantic similarity (Kim, Kim, and Lee 2019). In fact, Wu and Cunningham (2015, 474) argued that "in contrast to traditional encyclopedias, where authority derives from expert contributors, Wikipedia depends on collaboration and consensus to produce quality articles." This collaboration is based on the famous "wisdom of the crowds" principle, under which groups of ordinary mass can be "remarkably intelligent" (Surowiecki and Silverman 2007). Mostafa (2013aMostafa ( , 2013b) ) found that information and opinions expressed on the web are generally easy to understand and to act upon because of their reliable and affective nature. However, although web information has been investigated extensively (Cai et al. 2010;Leong, Lee, and Mak 2012;Wallace 2018;Kosterich and Weber 2019), no previous research has focused solely on analyzing halal food behavior on Wikipedia. Thus, we argue that the current research makes multiple contributions to the existing literature. First, we believe that this is the first study to use Wikipedia to investigate halal food search behavior. By examining the structure and dynamics of halal food search behavior, we contribute to the wider discussion on online information brokerage/gatekeeping behavior (DiMaggio et al. 2001;Vos and Heinderyckx 2015). Second, we believe that by investigating halal food search behavior, we add depth to the knowledge base on online religious-based consumption. In fact, Levitt (2012) argued that researchers investigating the link between religion and markets should focus on linkages and networks rather than bounded sites of religious practice. Finally, by focusing solely on analyzing halal food search behavior data, rather than on traditional offline survey data, we enrich the knowledge base of this under-represented area. More specifically, in this investigation we aim to find an answer to the following two major research questions:

RQ1. What are the major foraging strategies applied by halal food consumers in their search for halal food topics? RQ2. Do halal food topics tend to form distinct communities/clusters within the halal food Wikipedia network?

To find answers to our research questions, we apply a novel method based on data mining and social network analysis (SNA) in order to examine halal food search pathways and halal food Wikipedia network's structure, its relationship properties, its topics pattern of communications, and its different communities. Several scholars have recently advocated the need for adopting a "relational turn" in investigating online user-generated content (Wallace 2018, 218). The imperative to adopt a relational turn is theoretically based on the SNA paradigm, which argues that there is a need to move from methodologies that focus on attributive categorization into socio-economic status, race, etc. to focus rather on social ties among social actors (Wasche et al. 2017). In response to this call, we apply data mining and SNA, which are reliable and flexible quantitative methodologies that have been found to be useful in uncovering and mapping out structural attributes of communication networks as well as illuminating how information is exchanged (Scott 2013). By implementing such approach in this study, we emphasize the connectivity-based formation of the halal food Wikipedia network as we investigate the overall structure of the network and its major communities.

This paper is organized as follows. Next section reviews briefly the literature on Wikipedia's navigational strategies, its credibility and usage a proxy for offline phenomena. The following section deals with the method used to conduct the analysis. In this section issues related to research design and research methods are presented. In Section four results are presented along with robustness checks. Section five discusses research findings and presents major research implications. This section also presents research limitations and explores avenues for future research.

paragraphs: Although Wikipedia is the largest user-contributed online encyclopedia in the world (Kim, Kim, and Lee 2019), academic research on Wikipedia "has been predominantly technical, focusing, for instance, on the size, reliability, and creation process of encyclopedic content" (Gobel and Munzert 2018, 157). An extensive scholarly literature review on Wikipedia has revealed three distinct streams of research. One stream of research has investigated the different navigational strategies followed by users. A second stream of research has questioned the credibility of Wikipedia, while a third line of research has investigated the encyclopedia's usage as an indicator or predictor of offline phenomena. In the following subsections, we discuss in some detail the different research areas identified.

sections.paper_id: Mostafa - 2022 - Mining halal food search pathways down the Wikipedia’s rabbit hole
paragraphs: Computational cognitive models have been extensively used to model Wikipedia navigation. For example, using the information foraging concept (Pirolli and Card 1999), Chi (2001) argued that "information scent", borrowed from the optimal animal foraging theory in biology, can be used to guide users' search for patches of information. Pirolli and Fu (2003) developed a cognitive model known in the literature as the SNIF-ACT model. This model aims at analyzing different navigation choices selected by users in their navigation between webpages. Rodi, Loreto, and Tria (2017) modeled Wikipedia users' navigation using a stochastic memoryless process and argued that such statistical models are useful in simulating human navigation at the page level. Ratkiewicz et al. (2010) investigated Wikipedia users browsing activities over several months. The study was based on traffic flow gathered locally.

Similarly, some studies have examined Wikipedia's navigational strategies via speciallydesigned games which track the navigation path followed by players under some spatial and temporal constraints (West, Pineau, and Precup 2009;West, Paranjape, and Leskovec 2015). Studies comparing navigation models against real human search process found that such models do indeed mimic human navigation patterns (Lamprecht et al. 2015).

sections.paper_id: Mostafa - 2022 - Mining halal food search pathways down the Wikipedia’s rabbit hole
paragraphs: Since Wikipedia allows anyone to edit and create articles, a critical issue related to authoritative reference source has emerged (Korfiatis, Poulos, and Bokos 2006). The debate on whether Wikipedia is a credible source of information has led to a plethora of research examining the encyclopedia's reliability (Koppen, Phillips, and Papageorgiou 2015), quality (Arazy et al. 2011), accuracy (Brown 2011), content validity (Wallace and Fleet 2005), impact (Xu and Zhang 2013) and coverage (Keegan, Gergle, and Contractor 2013). However, research evaluating the Wikipedia's credibility compared to expertproduced encyclopedias suggests that the actual difference is trivial. For example, Tomaszewski and MacDonald (2016) found that the use of Wikipedia citations in refereed journals has been increasing. A Nature research team also found that Wikipedia's scientific information is reliable and accurate (Giles 2006). In a similar vein, several studies have shown that user-generated entries in Wikipedia to be as accurate and reputable as expert-created encyclopedias such as the Encyclopedia Britannica (Flanagin and Metzger 2011;Dewald 2014). In a study investigating citations to Wikipedia in chemistry journals, Brazzeal (2011) found that although "a small percentage of all articles contained a citation to Wikipedia, it is, in fact, being cited as a credible information source in articles in major chemistry journals."

sections.header_raw: Wikipedia as a proxy of offline phenomena
sections.header_norm: wikipedia as a proxy of offline phenomena
paragraphs: Wikipedia was extensively used as an indicator of a plethora of offline phenomena. For example, Yang and Lai (2011) have explored how attitudes toward Wikipedia influence knowledge-sharing behavior. Yun and Gloor (2015) found a link between a firm's centrality position in Wikipedia and its innovation capability. Similarly, Moat et al. (2013) examined the relationship between Wikipedia's financial topics' edits and stock market performance. Mestyan, Yasseri, and Kertesz (2013) used Wikipedia data to predict box office revenues. Public anxiety related to swine flu was also linked to Wikipedia visits (Tausczik et al. (2012). Zant et al. (2018a) analyzed major world terrorist networks using a reduced Google matrix of Wikipedia. Park et al. (2015) used Wikipedia to classify cultural values among nations based on the editors' collaboration system. Shi et al. (2019) analyzed millions of Wikipedia edits to study the effect of team composition on performance. In a similar vein, Gobel and Munzert (2018) explored how politicians tend to revise their biographies on Wikipedia as a means to appeal to their constituencies. Yasseri and Bright (2015) argued that the election outcomes can be predicted using Wikipedia data. The mass salience of the American Supreme Court decisions was also measured using Wikipedia (Wilkerson 2015). Finally, Wikipedia has been used to investigate painters, universities, and cancerous disease networks (Coquide et al. 2019;Rollin, Lages, and Shepelyansky 2019;Zant et al. 2018b).

From this brief literature review, we find that although several studies have investigated Wikipedia using plethora of methodologies, no previous studies have implemented information network analysis to investigate Wikipedia's halal food search behavior. By filling this research gap, we contribute to the wider discussion on online information brokerage/gatekeeping behavior (DiMaggio et al. 2001;Vos and Heinderyckx 2015).

Wikipedia was extensively used as an indicator of a plethora of offline phenomena. For example, Yang and Lai (2011) have explored how attitudes toward Wikipedia influence knowledge-sharing behavior. Yun and Gloor (2015) found a link between a firm's centrality position in Wikipedia and its innovation capability. Similarly, Moat et al. (2013) examined the relationship between Wikipedia's financial topics' edits and stock market performance. Mestyan, Yasseri, and Kertesz (2013) used Wikipedia data to predict box office revenues. Public anxiety related to swine flu was also linked to Wikipedia visits (Tausczik et al. (2012). Zant et al. (2018a) analyzed major world terrorist networks using a reduced Google matrix of Wikipedia. Park et al. In a similar vein, Gobel and Munzert (2018) explored how politicians tend to revise their biographies on Wikipedia as a means to appeal to their constituencies. sections.paper_id: Mostafa - 2022 - Mining halal food search pathways down the Wikipedia’s rabbit hole
paragraphs: In this study, we follow the two-step methodology suggested by Lamprecht et al. (2017) because it has been used successfully in similar research (Drias, Kechid, and Pasi 2016). The following steps were applied to guide the analyses reported in the article:

(1) All clicks within an entire month of the English Wikipedia were analyzed. Since such clicks usually reconstruct trails or traces of user activity, the analysis allows us to "gain insight into unrestricted free-form clicking behavior on Wikipedia, covering all usage scenarios" (Lamprecht et al. 2017, 33). (2) An information network was constructed whereby topics navigated were connected via a sociogram whenever they refer to each other. Statistical analysis and robustness checks were conducted on the Wikipedia halal food topics network by comparing it against a random realization of the Erdős-Rényi network models.

To conduct the analysis, several packages within the R version 3.6 environment (R Development Core Team 2019) were used, including tidyverse, readr, plyr, pageviews, networkD3,, and wikipediatrend. To reduce computational burden, we parsed the data using SQL in R. The function read.csv.sql() from the sqldf library was implemented to read the data. For the network visualization, the igraph package version 1.2.4 (Csardi and Nepusz 2006) was used. This software was selected because it is capable of handling large social network graphs and calculating advanced network metrics efficiently. In the following subsections we discuss in some detail the methodological process mentioned above.

sections.paper_id: Mostafa - 2022 - Mining halal food search pathways down the Wikipedia’s rabbit hole
paragraphs: The aggregate Wikipedia traffic data is maintained by the Wikimedia Foundation, which allows the general public to access for non-commercial use through a suite of publicly accessible tools (Wulczyn and Taraborelli 2015). Initially, the dataset was released sporadically and in different formats. However, starting November 2017, the format was released each month in a standardized format. This dataset, known in literature as the Wikipedia clickstream records, aggregates clicks from all types of visits in the form of click cunts. Rodi, Loreto, and Tria (2017, 2) stated that the Wikipedia clickstream "represents a very important step forward to address the question of the navigation strategies of users in information networks." Lamprecht et al. (2017) argued that, since the user navigating Wikipedia cannot be identified, the clickstream data make it possible to study user's behavior non-reactively, which means that the user behavior will not be subject to change if they knew that they were being recorded. Jabaley et al. (2019) stated that the clickstream data can be used to trace traffic patterns leading to, or coming from, a certain Wikipedia page. Following Lamprecht et al. (2017), we used the latest available data for August 2019 after excluding any external webpages cited in Wikipedia articles. The clickstream used in this study is considered "big data" as it includes more than 25 million distinct requests of articles for a total of over 6 billion clicks together with their referrers or the webpages from which requests were performed.

sections.paper_id: Mostafa - 2022 - Mining halal food search pathways down the Wikipedia’s rabbit hole
paragraphs: Although the terms "social networks" and "information networks" are used interchangeably in the literature (Korfiatis, Poulos, and Bokos 2006), Lamprecht et al. (2017) argued that information networks imply different navigational characteristics. For example, while several actors interact in navigating a social network, an information network is only navigated by a single agent. However, while the single user involved in the Wikipedia search stays the same throughout the search process, the selection of a certain link to click is taken independently at each step. Thus, Wikipedia can be thought of as an information network in which each article includes references to other articles as well as external references. Thus, each article can be regarded as a node or vertex in the network linked by arcs or edges forming the internal connections between articles. Following Ponza, Ferragina, and Chakrabarti (Forthcoming), we model the Wikipedia halal food information network as a labeled graph, in which Wikipedia pages/articles describing entities are represented by nodes, while hyperlinks between Wikipedia pages are represented by edges. Thus, the information network was constructed such that if article i refers to hyperlink j, then the former is connected to the latter and the direction of the tie is from i to j. The weight of the edge equals the number of times that i refers to j.

sections.paper_id: Mostafa - 2022 - Mining halal food search pathways down the Wikipedia’s rabbit hole
Mostafa - 2022 - Mining halal food search pathways down the Wikipedia’s rabbit hole
paragraphs: Before analyzing the clickstream data, we used the wikipediatrend package (Meissner 2014) within the R programming environment to plot the halal food page-view over time. Figure 1 shows the time series distribution of the daily Wikipedia page-views from 2008 to 2019. From the plot, it is clear that the time series seems to be nonstationary, with heavy spikes that might represent either a random noise or a link to an offline phenomenon. Ratkiewicz et al. (2010, 295) argued that bursts in Wikipedia article views might be linked to "appropriately chosen queries on Google Trends, suggesting that these bursts are often driven by external event." To further analyze this, we decomposed the time series into its major components using the LOESS method (Cleveland et al. 1990). Figure 2 shows the decomposed normalized data components for the English Wikipedia page-views related to halal food. The graph exhibits considerable volatility over time with a strong seasonal component. However, apart from a sudden dip around 2012-2013, the trend seems to be fairly stable around 3000 daily views.

Having analyzed the general pattern of Wikipedia halal food page-view over time, we turn now to focus on the clickstream dataset. We start by considering the search patterns of the incoming traffic to the halal food article. This scenario is similar to someone who was reading a certain Wikipedia article and only then decides to click on the halal food article. Figure 3 shows such scenario using a Sankey diagram. A Sankey diagram shows the flow trend between two or more entities (Sankey 1898). The boxes and arrows widths in a Sankey diagram are proportional to the related quantity (Soundararajan, Ho, and Su 2014). From the graph, we see that most of the readers come to the article either from a non-specified search engine or from some other internal Wikipedia search, which is a nice example of falling into the Wikipedia rabbit hole-a metaphor based on the fairy tale Alice's Adventure in Wonderland, in which the protagonist embarks on an odyssey as she follows the White Rabbit into his burrow. However, some of the halal food incoming traffic emanate from very specific searches such as "Dhabihah" (Arabic for slaughtered animal) or "Jhatka" (Hindi and Urdu for stunning an animal). However, as we can see from the graph, this pattern is less common. Figure 4 shows the opposite scenario of the outgoing traffic. In this graph, we plot Wikipedia readers who begin their journey by the Wikipedia's halal food article to see what they click on next. In this scenario, we see that the readers are less prone to fall into the rabbit hole. In fact, the readers continue to dig deep for closely related articles such as "Kashrut" (Hebrew for Jewish laws related to food suitability) and "Makruh" (Arabic for disliked but permitted food). Although the tendency here is more toward deeper levels of specialized knowledge, few searches lead to more general articles such as "People of the Book" (Koranic reference to Christians and Jews). From the Sankey diagrams, it is clear that search engines account for the majority of known external referrals. Similar results were reported by Jabaley et al. (2019).

sections.paper_id: Mostafa - 2022 - Mining halal food search pathways down the Wikipedia’s rabbit hole
paragraphs: Figure 5 shows the Wikipedia halal food information network. It is a directed graph in which the nodes represent Wikipedia topics related to halal food, while the edges represent hyperlinks' relationships. The network includes 25 nodes or topics linked by 99 hyperlinks or edges. The network provides a snapshot of more central topics, as well as the ones occupying a more peripheral role.

A closer inspection of the graph reveals that the typology of the network reflects a hub-andspoke structure typical of small world networks (Milgram 1967;Watts and Strogatz 1998). This structure indicates the existence of few key topics which dominate the network and play an important role in information diffusion/brokerage through the network, forming bridges between other key clusters in the network (Park et al. 2015). It has been argued that smallworld networks are characterized by small groups of nodes that are well connected and highly clustered, which "let messages move through the network effectively" (Shirky 2008, 215). Smith andGraham (2019, 1318) argued that small-world networks are also robust since "randomly removing nodes from the network will not significantly impact the effectiveness and dynamics of the network."

The Wikipedia halal food information network reflects also what is sometimes called the Matthew Effect in sociology-a mechanism akin to the preferential attachment in which a small number of hubs have larger number of connections compared to the rest of actors. Newman and Girvan (2004) argued that new links in a large network are preferably attached to well-connected nodes. Consequently, connectivity in highly connected nodes augments faster than less-connected nodes. This phenomenon is known in the literature as the power-law mechanism, which is a variation of the rich-get-richer model in which just a few nodes form the core of the cluster in which they are located (Himelboim, Smith, and Shneiderman 2013). Preferential attachment has been found in myriad social networks, including academic work citations, e-mails, sexual relationships, diseases and World Wide Web hyperlinks (Barabasi et al. 2000;Doods, Muhamad, and Wattas 2003;Himelboim and Han 2014).

The Wikipedia halal food information network reflects also what is sometimes called the Matthew Effect in sociology-a mechanism akin to the preferential attachment in which a small number of hubs have larger number of connections compared to the rest of actors. Newman and Girvan (2004) argued that new links in a large network are preferably attached to well-connected nodes. Consequently, connectivity in highly connected nodes augments faster than less-connected nodes. This phenomenon is known in the literature as the power-law mechanism, which is a variation of the rich-get-richer model in which just a few nodes form the core of the cluster in which they are located (Himelboim, Smith, and Shneiderman 2013). paragraphs: There are various statistical tools that can be used to determine how well connected an article is within an information network. The three most widely used metrics are degree, closeness, and betweenness centrality (Gruzd, Wellman, and Takhteyev 2011). An article with a high degree of centrality is considered influencer because it directly reaches many other articles without the need to rely on some other intermediate articles (Theocharis 2013). Following Barisione, Michailidou, and Airoldi (2019), we employed in-degree as a prime measure of articles' influence. Thus, while isolated articles have a zero in-degree, those largely well connected represent central hubs in the Wikipedia halal food information network. However, it should be noted that while in-degree centrality can be used to determine an article's local prominence, this metric does not measure an article's indirect ties in the network. To capture global influence, Bodendorf and Kaiser (2009) proposed two other metrics, namely closeness centrality and betweenness centrality. Closeness centrality estimates how close an article is to all other articles in the network. Thus, it can be regarded as a measure of node's efficiency in spreading information to other nodes in the network. Finally, betweenness centrality measures how often a given article or node falls along the shortest geodesic path between two other articles (Borgatti, Everett, and Johnson 2013). In terms of the Wikipedia halal food information network, an article with high betweenness centrality can play effectively the role of information brokerage as such article can easily broker information between two articles that do not connect directly with each other.

Figure 6 shows the in-degree, closeness, and betweenness distributions in the Wikipedia halal food information network. From the graph, we see that only a few articles are in fact influencers in the network. This result is in line with the findings of the traditional influence theories (Katz 1957). Recent empirical social media research has reported similar results. For example, Gonzalez-Bailon and Wang (2016) found that only a few actors on Twitter protest movements form an elite who broker information and are more often mentioned and retweeted.

To check the robustness of the results, we conducted a simulation to compare the Wikipedia halal food information network against an equivalent Erdős-Rényi random network. Although a random network might be quite unusual in information systems as it is hard to imagine a system that would be connected randomly, this structure is usually applied by researchers as an appropriate null hypothesis network (Hartvigsen 2011). Following Dang et al. (2019), we started by generating 1000 simulations of an Erdős-Rényi random network with the same node, edge, and degree size. Table 1 reports both networks nodes and edges' sizes, density, along with other major metrics for the Erdős-Rényi random network. By comparing the metrics of the Wikipedia halal food information network and the classical Erdős-Rényi random network models, we see that the Wikipedia halal food information network density was particularly low (0.165), indicating that the network has a relatively limited number of interactions, with only a few articles in the network are, to a certain degree, densely interconnected with one another. Reciprocity was high (0.565). However, Erdős-Rényi random network's reciprocity was 1. Although transitivity was not particularly high (0.384), it was relatively higher than the Erdős-Rényi random network transitivity metric (0.293). Transitivity "refers to the probability of balanced triads in a network, such that if A and B are connected, and B and C are connected, then A and C are also connected" (Clifton and Webster 2017, 448). Modularity metric, which measures the quality of clustering divisions, was 0.371 (compared to a modularity of 0.167 for the Erdős-Rényi random network). Although Newman and Girvan (2004) suggest 0.6 as a threshold for detecting meaningfully distinct clusters, Wang (2012) has shown that a modularity of 0.4 is a sufficient threshold for obtaining distinct clusters. Thus, our results indicate that the Wikipedia halal food network comprises multiple distinct communities of densely connected actors.

sections.paper_id: Mostafa - 2022 - Mining halal food search pathways down the Wikipedia’s rabbit hole
paragraphs: Halal food subgroups or clusters maybe conceptualized as Wikipedia communities because each of such clusters form a subgroup of highly interconnected actors who share the same interests/ideological standpoints and occupy the same virtual space. In this study, we use the cluster_walktrap function in the igraph package to implement the walktrap community detection algorithm (Pons andLatapy 2005, 2006). This algorithm was selected because it can efficiently detect different dimensions of variables even when the dimensions are highly correlated (Delage et al. 2017). The walktrap algorithm follows a random walk process under the assumption that random walks will eventually get "trapped" within the communities (Hoffman et al. 2018). Applying this algorithm resulted in identifying three major halal food Wikipedia clusters as shown in Figure 7. In this graph connected nodes located in different clusters indicate mediated relationships. Figure 8 zooms in on the three major clusters detected using the walktrap algorithm. From Figure 8, it is clear that the nodes in major communities tend to interact with each other, indicating a strong "homophily effect" (McPherson, Smith-Lovin, and Cook 2001). This effect occurs when actors in an environment similar to a virtual room deal with topics of common interest or discuss a common agenda (Findlay and van Rensburg 2018).

To understand what might be driving halal food discourse of the identified communities detected, we performed topic modeling on some halal food Wikipedia topics. Figure 9 shows the probabilities of the top terms per topic discussed in four Wikipedia's halal food topics. Since all the terms discussed within each topic seem somehow related, no topics were identified as "junk" topics (Nikolenko, Koltcov, and Koltsova 2017). The topics seem to deal with issues related to halal certification in Europe, Dhabihah, Jewish food laws and a comparison between Islamic and Jewish dietary laws. For example, topic three deals with issues related to halal food authenticity and certification in Europe and includes terms such as "animal slaughter", "certification", "stunning" and the "UK".

sections.paper_id: Mostafa - 2022 - Mining halal food search pathways down the Wikipedia’s rabbit hole
paragraphs: Using the recently released clickstream data accounting for a total of around 6-billionpage requests to the Wikipedia servers, this study investigated halal food navigation strategies over an entire month. Analyzing Wikipedia's clickstream data related to halal food can provide insights on how visible halal food topic is among a large network of topics as the clickstream data covers all possible usage scenarios, including learning about concepts, looking up facts, reading to pass time, among others. We argue that halal food digital information networks can play an important role in diminishing geographical constraints by reducing the transaction cost of information (Coiera 2000), which can empower different actors to extend their ideas beyond traditional geographic boundaries.

Examining clickstream data for halal food search patterns can help to better elucidate user behavior driving such information-seeking patterns. For example, we found that, compared to outgoing traffic analysis, halal food search patterns of the incoming traffic reveal that readers are more prone to fall into the Wikipedia rabbit hole. Using techniques borrowed from social network analysis (SNA), our analysis suggests that the Wikipedia halal food network, despite its high level of activity and relative size, is indeed a realization of a sparse hub-and-periphery network structure. This result is very important because it indicates that only few central articles within close-knit communities control the flow of halal food information. Such topics are often called "influencers" because they tend to create widely-read and consulted content (Marwick 2014;Senft 2008;Yerasani et al. 2019). Because influencers occupy central position in the network, we argue that they can play an important role in bridging structural holes in the network. Thus, influencers might be regarded as information brokers who connect different groups of articles in the network, which gives them a "structural advantage" over other actors (Burt 1999).

Despite the contribution of this study, there are some limitations. First, previous research has found that Internet data are generally susceptible to demographic biases such as overrepresentation of younger cohorts (Longley and Adnan 2016) and contribution bias caused by the fact that large percentage of contents are generated by a small proportion of users (Morstatter et al. 2013). Nevertheless, we believe that such data are characterized by "a high temporal granularity that is international in scale" (Lloyd and Cheshire 2016, 109). We also believe that due to the huge volume available, Wikipedia data are characterized by large representation (Adnan, Lansley, and Longley 2013). Future research may test whether results of this study hold by comparing traditional search approaches in offline encyclopedia against Wikipedia search strategies. Second, this research has investigated Wikipedia's halal food network of all entries regardless of their content. However, the halal food network structure may vary according to how halal food is framed in the Wikipedia's text. Thus, future research should examine whether such content plays a role in shaping the search path or determine which topic attains a central position in the network. For example, are myths about halal food and animal slaughter disseminated the same way as real stories about genuine halal food consumption? Finally, it should be noted that although we successfully analyzed Wikipedia's information diffusion, our results do not reveal the underlying dynamics behind forming halal food content. Future qualitative research should reveal the reasons behind forming halal food content, which may allow stakeholders to gain overall insights into the underlying causes of forming positive or negative opinions regarding halal food.

Analyzing Wikipedia's clickstream data related to halal food can provide insights on how visible halal food topic is among a large network of topics as the clickstream data covers all possible usage scenarios, including learning about concepts, looking up facts, reading to pass time, among others. We argue that halal food digital information networks can play an important role in diminishing geographical constraints by reducing the transaction cost of information (Coiera 2000), which can empower different actors to extend their ideas beyond traditional geographic boundaries.
sections.preview_tail: For example, are myths about halal food and animal slaughter disseminated the same way as real stories about genuine halal food consumption? Finally, it should be noted that although we successfully analyzed Wikipedia's information diffusion, our results do not reveal the underlying dynamics behind forming halal food content. Future qualitative research should reveal the reasons behind forming halal food content, which may allow stakeholders to gain overall insights into the underlying causes of forming positive or negative opinions regarding halal food.
